summary:
Trajan (r. 98–117 AD) was Rome's greatest imperial conqueror — the emperor under whom the Roman Empire reached its maximum territorial extent, conquering Dacia (modern Romania) in two campaigns (101–102 and 105–107 AD) and briefly adding Mesopotamia and Armenia in his Parthian campaign. His military success made him the archetype of the good emperor-general: later emperors were wished 'felicior Augusto, melior Traiano' (luckier than Augustus, better than Trajan).

Trajan's Column (dedicated 113 AD) in his forum is one of antiquity's most extraordinary monuments — a 38-meter marble column with a continuous helical relief narrative of the Dacian Wars spiraling upward, depicting approximately 2,500 figures in 155 distinct scenes. It served simultaneously as a war memorial, a record of Roman military engineering and tactics, and as the funerary monument for Trajan's ashes placed in its base. Trajan's conquest of Dacia brought enormous wealth: Dacia's gold and silver mines contributed massive resources to the Roman treasury, funding his massive building programs in Rome (Trajan's Market, Trajan's Forum, Trajan's Column). The [[event:2113|Dacian Wars Trajan]] details these campaigns specifically.
